coredata.dc:title: Eco-friendly modification of a regenerated cellulose based film by silicon, carbon and N-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Cuevas, A.
Campos, B.B.
Romero, R.
Algarra, M.
Vázquez, Mª.I.
Benavente, J.
coredata.dc:description: Modification of a regenerated cellulose thin film by inclusion of different non-toxic nanodots (silicon-dots (SiDs), carbon-dots (CDs)) or nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs)) by aqueous nanodots solution immersion was performed. Nanodots presence into the cellulosic structure was evidenced by confocal microscopy images at different depth and changes in film mechanical, electrical and optical parameters. Our results reveal that the inclusion of the different nanodots in the cellulosic support increases, indifferent percentages, the mechanical resistance and electrical conductivity of modified films, but they hardly affect light transmittance. Particularly, modification with N-CDs largely favored film conductivity due to the presence of the higher number of charged functional groups (CNH2 and OCNH2) groups) on N-CDs surface, allowin gus the attainment of a flexible, fluorescent and transparent high conductive eco-friendly film. In fact, the non-toxic character of both support-film and nanodots, endorses the use of these new nano-engineering films in biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulosic films
Carbon nanodots
Spectroscopy ellipsometry
Transmittance

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: ARDITI-Agência Regional para o Desenvolvimento da Investigação Tecnologia e Inovação
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Agência Regional para o Desenvolvimento da Investigação, Tecnologia e Inovação

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: One of the authors (M.A.) thanks to the project CTQ2015-68951-C3-3-R (MINECO, Spain) and to ARDITI-Agência Regional para o Desenvolvimento da Investigação Tecnologia e Inovação is also acknowledged through the project M1420-01-0145-FEDER-000005-CQM+ (Madeira 14-20 Program). We also thank to the Supercomputing and Bioinnovation Center and Junta de Andalucía (PAI group FQM 258) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) TEM image of the obtained N-CDs (inset: HR-TEM image of N-CDs; scale bar of 200 nm). B) comparison of the average of the different nanodots studied in this work. FE-SEM image (scale bar of 500 nm) of C) CDs, D) N-CDs and E) SiDs on the surface of the cellulose support (RC-4).
Confocal fluorescence images (excitation at 405 nm). A) RC-4 film as control image; B) RC-4/N-CDs film, C) RC-4/CDs film, D) RC-4/SiDs film.
Impedance spectroscopy diagrams (Bode plots) for the different films: RC-4 (□), RC-4/SiDs (♦), RC-4/CDs (▲) and RC-4/N-CDs (●). (a) Zreal vs frequency; (b) – Zimg vs frequency.

Wavelength dependence of parameter Y (A) and Δ (B) for original RC-4 film (black) and modified RC-4/SiDs (red), RC-4/CDs (blue) and RC-4/NCDs (green) films. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

Comparison of UV-VIS-NIR transmittance spectra for the cellulose substrate RC-4 (black) and for the RC-4/SiQDs (red), RC-4/CDs (blue), RC-4/N-CQDs (green) modified films (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
Values of elastic modulus (Y) and electrical conductivity (σ) for the studied films.
Values of refraction index (n), extinction coefficient (k) and transmittance at 632.8 nm and 1000 nm, for the studied films.

head.ce:title: Eco-friendly modification of a regenerated cellulose based film by silicon, carbon and N-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Unidad de Nanotecnología, Centro de Supercomputación y Bioinnovación, Universidad de Málaga, E-29071 Málaga, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centro de Investigação em Química, Departamento de Química, Faculdade de Ciências da Universidade do Porto, 4169-007 Porto, Portugal

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculdade de Ciências da Universidade do Porto

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CQM-Centro de Química da Madeira, Universidade da Madeira, Campus da Penteada 9020-105 Funchal, Portugal

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Grupo de Caracterización Electrocinética en Membranas e Interfases, Dpto. Física Aplicada I, Facultad de Ciencias, Universidad de Málaga, 29071 Málaga, Spain

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Grupo de Caracterización Electrocinética en Membranas e Interfases

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Departamento de Química Inorgánica, Facultad de Ciencias, Universidad de Málaga, Campus de Teatinos s/n, 29071 Málaga, Spain.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Modification of a regenerated cellulose thin film by inclusion of different non-toxic nanodots (silicon-dots (SiDs), carbon-dots (CDs)) or nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs)) by aqueous nanodots solution immersion was performed. Nanodots presence into the cellulosic structure was evidenced by confocal microscopy images at different depth and changes in film mechanical, electrical and optical parameters. Our results reveal that the inclusion of the different nanodots in the cellulosic support increases, indifferent percentages, the mechanical resistance and electrical conductivity of modified films, but they hardly affect light transmittance. Particularly, modification with N-CDs largely favored film conductivity due to the presence of the higher number of charged functional groups (CNH2 and OCNH2) groups) on N-CDs surface, allowin gus the attainment of a flexible, fluorescent and transparent high conductive eco-friendly film. In fact, the non-toxic character of both support-film and nanodots, endorses the use of these new nano-engineering films in biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Regenerated cellulose (RC) is a natural polymer widely studied due to its numerous properties such as renewable sources, biodegradability, transparency, flexibility, low cost and versatility, since it can be rather easily modified, which made of RC a key material for different applications (Garrison, Murawsky, & Quirino, 2016; Klemm et al., 2011; Lin & Dufresne, 2014; Moon, Martini, Nairn, Simonsen, & Youngblood, 2011). Two of these characteristics, its inherent biocompatibility and high transparency, make of RC an important chemical resource for technological applications such as electronic components, consumer products and aerospace in near future (Dutta et al., 2017; Mohanty, Misra, & Drzal, 2005; Pérez-Madrigal, Edo, & Alemán, 2016; Wang, Lu, & Zhang, 2016). In fact, new applications based on some of those properties of RC are nowadays being explored and developed to create cellulosic materials with added value, such as processing of cellulose-based materials with ionic liquids (Colburn, Wanninayake, Kim, & Bhattacharyya, 2018; Habibi, Lucia, & Rojas, 2010; Tsioptsias, Stefopoulos, Kokkinomalis, Papadopoulou, & Panayiotou, 2008; Wang, Gurau, & Rogers, 2012).

Functionalization of RC-films with CdSe/ZnS or CdS quantum dots, for improving their optical properties, has been performed by procedures involving chemical reactions (Abitbol et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2016). However, RC film modification by most simple methods (without chemical reaction), such as its immersion in aqueous solutions of the modifying agents for the attainment of hybrids cellulosic nanocomposites with improved properties, can be of interest. In this context, it has already been demonstrated in different papers that the high elastic and hydrophilic character of RC films allows the inclusion of different kinds of nanoparticles (lipid-NPs or Ag-NPs) or other modifying elements such as hydroxyapatite or dendrimers, for the attainment of hybrids cellulosic nanocomposites with improved properties (Algarra, Campos et al., 2014; Benavente et al., 2017; Sarkar et al., 2018; Vázquez et al., 2011).

In fact, our group has already studied the incorporation of fluorescent CdSe quantum dots (CdSe-QDs) coated by a thiol DAB dendrimer into a RC film (Algarra et al., 2013; Campos et al., 2016), being the reduction in the toxicity of the CdSe-QDs the main reason for dendrimer coverage (Barman et al., 2018), although it might also favour the link of the QDs with the cellulose chains to ensure their permanence into the structure of the RC film even in adverse conditions, that it, in contact with aqueous solutions in the case electrochemical applications. However, the presence of heavy atoms in the modifying agents limits the ecological benefits of the RC support, reducing the field of applicability of the nano-engineering cellulosic films. In fact, the stability of modified RC-based films using less toxic QDs such as silicon derivatives (SiDs), both bare and functionalized with PAMAM-OH dendrimer, has already been confirmed, and used for chemical sensing respectively (Campos, Abellan et al., 2015, 2016; Vázquez, Algarra, & Benavente, 2015).

Since carbon materials based on carbon dots (CDs) are also biocompatible and non-toxic nanomaterials, they might be considered as a metal free alternative for innovative valorization of RC-films. In fact, functionalization of cellulosic films by CDs and sulphur or nitrogen doped carbon dots (S-CDs and N-CDs, respectively) has already been indicated (Zheng & Yan, 2015). CDs are fluorescent spherical nanoparticles (<10 nm) with a carbonaceous core containing multiple oxygen species, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and aldehyde groups (Guo et al., 2017; Zhang & Yu, 2016).

To improve the quantum yield (QY) of the CDs, and for surface passivation reason, they are usually doped with heteroatoms, such as N, P or B; The N-doped CDs (N-CDs) seem to be the most efficient approach to get QY increase (Campos, Algarra et al., 2015; Shen et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2018; Travlou et al., 2018). In this context, the inclusion of CDs into the structure of the RC films should reduce the anisotropy of the modified cellulosic material, while their modification using carbon nanoparticles doped with nitrogen atoms (N-CDs) should improve the photo-physical properties and biocompatibility of the films compared with other doped carbon dots (Sun, Yang, Wang, & Wang, 2018). Since carbon dots (CDs) are non-toxic nanomaterials, they might be considered as a metal free alternative for innovative valorization of RC-films.

On the other hand, the coating effect of carbon dots to enhance luminescent properties of different matrix has been described. Particularly, CDs dispersed on carboxymethyl cellulose films were developed for sunlight (You, Zhang, Liu, & Lei, 2016), while N-CDs were loaded in bacterial cellulose, obtaining a nanocomposite able to be used as a chemical sensor for Fe3+ ions (Lv et al., 2017). Cellulose nanofibrils and polymer films (PVA matrix) were surface functionalized by using covalent process to enhance the quantum yield of fluorescence (Junka, Guo, Ilari, Laine, & Rojas, 2014; Li et al., 2015), while other researchers, using different CDs precursors, obtained tuneable luminescent polymer matrix-based polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) (Bhunia, Nandi, Shikler, & Jelinek, 2016). Poly(methyl methacrylate) nanocomposites coated by CDs were used to obtain white light emitting system when loaded with lanthanides (Chen & Feng, 2015).

This work reports the possibility of inclusion of different types of nano-dots into the structure of a RC-film by simple dip coating method, and their effects on different characteristic membrane materials. Our results show that the modified films are transparent and luminescent (in visible region), with improved electrical conductivity and higher mechanical strength that the support film. Moreover, since the nanodots used for film modification have not any metal atom, they can be considered eco-friendly materials, and be used as platforms for technological and bioengineering systems.

Silicon quantum dots (SiDs), carbon and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs and N-CDs) were prepared according to the methodology described in previous papers (Campos, Abellan et al., 2015; Campos, Algarra et al., 2015; Algarra, Vázquez et al., 2014) by hydrothermal approach. Briefly, to obtain SiDs, APTES (3-Aminopropyl)triethoxysilane, 99%) was heat at 200 °C for 3 h, while CDs were obtained from lactose, following the same procedure (at 80 °C) but using H2SO4 as oxidant agent for 24 h. N-CDs were obtained by a different way, in this case lactose (1 M) was treated with NH3 (25%) and thermally heat at 100 °C for 3 h, and after this it was neutralized with HCl (1 M). All reactions were carried out in a teflon-equipped stainless-steel and all nanoparticles solutions obtained were filtered and dialyzed against water for 24 h. Atomic concentrations of the characteristic elements found on the surface of the different nanodots (from XPS analysis) are given as Supplementary Information (Table 1S).

A flat regenerated cellulose (RC) film from Cellophane Española, S.A. (Burgos, Spain), with a RC content of 0.04 kg/m2 and a high degree of swollen, (83 ± 6) %, was used as support film (sample RC/4). The regenerated cellulose (cellophane) film used in this work seems to be obtained from the alkali/CS2 treatment, and both FTIR and XPS spectra as well as atomic concentrations of the characteristic elements (from XPS analysis) are given as Supplementary Information (Figs. 1S and 2S, and Table 2S). Pieces of the RC/4 film were immersed for 24 h in aqueous solutions of SiDs, CDs or N-CDs, and left dried at room temperature (samples RC-4/SiDs, RC-4/CDs and RC-4/N-CDs).

Nanodots morphology was analysed by high-resolution transmission electronic microscopy (HR-TEM) using FEI Talos F200X equipment. A Helios Nanolab 650 dual beam microscope (FEI Company) equipped with an Elstar Schottky thermal field emitter was used for films surface analysis.

Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was performed with a Leica SP 5 II confocal laser scanning microscope (Leica, Heidelberg, Germany) and images taken at each 250 nm were processed using the LAS AF software. Small pieces of the modified samples were put on microscope slides under a coverslip and the fluorescence of the samples was studied by CLSM. For visualization of multispectral image data, the selected channel was associated with a color value on the display system, blue in this case, using a band pass filter (BP) of 515–565 nm.

Elastic characterization was performed by using a force digital gauge (Mark-T, ES20 model) connected to a computer, with a maximum tension of 100 N, length accurate of ± 0.01 mm and a strength rate of 10 mm/s.

Impedance spectroscopy (IS) measurements were performed in a test cell consisted of a Teflon support on which two Pt electrodes were placed and screwed down. The electrodes were connected to a Response Frequency Analyzer (Solartron 1260) and 100 data points were recorded for frequency ranging between 1 Hz and 107 Hz, at a maximum voltage of 0.01 V.

Spectroscopic Ellipsometry (SE) measurements were carried out with a spectroscopic ellipsometer (Sopra-Semilab GES-5E) using wavelengths in a range from 200 nm to 1000 nm at an incident angle of 70°. WinElli software from Sopra-Semilab was used for data fit. A Varian Cary 5000 spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA) provided with an integrating sphere of Spectralon was employed to record the transmittance spectra of the studied films over a wavelength interval of 200–1000 nm.

Fig. 1 A shows the TEM image of the well dispersed N-CDs nanoparticles obtained upon solvothermal treatment at 100 °C, where their spherical morphology is clearly observed; for comparison reasons, TEM images of SiDs and CDs are given as Supplementary Information (Fig. 3S and Table 3S). Fig. 1B shows a comparison of the average size of the different nanodots used in this work: SiDs (∼14 nm), CDs (∼7 nm) and N-CDs (∼ 40 nm). The significant higher size exhibited by the N-CDs when compared with the other two kinds of nanoparticles is attributed to an aggregation process, as a result of electrostatic interactions (Van der Waals force interactions) among hydrogen bonds, and it is related to the variety of functional groups on the surface of the N-CDs according to XPS results (Campos, Abellan et al., 2015; Campos, Algarra et al., 2015), contributing to for form high size nanoparticles.

Fig. 1C–E shows the FE-SEM images of the surface of the cellulosic films modified with the different NPs, where aggregations can be observed independently of the nanoparticles size. However, their inclusion into the cellulosic structure of the support-film might also be considered, and it will be analysed in the following sections. Both, the presence of the different nano-dots in the structure of the cellulosic-support film and their effects on diverse characteristic material parameters, can be determined by different characterization techniques such as confocal microscopy, strain-strength/elongation curves and by ellipsometry, impedance and transmittance spectroscopies.

The fluorescence of the different nanoparticles was detected by confocal microscopy. Fig. 2 A presents a scheme of the procedure to record the confocal images of the different films of 1 μ m2 area, at a given depth Z = 8 μm with respect to the surface plane (XY), when the samples were irradiated under a laser at 405 nm of excitation. These images show the significantly higher brightness of the RC-4/N-CDs film and it is associated to carbon NPs surface functionalization, which is mainly constituted by amine (NH2) and amide (CONH2) groups (previously analyzed and confirmed by XPS, Campos, Abellan et al., 2015; Campos, Algarra et al., 2015), while the moderate/low brightness exhibited by the films with the two other kinds of nanodots (CDs and SiDs) could be attribute to the presence of COOH coating groups (Fig. 2B–D, respectively). But, after analysing the different planes taken for RC-4/nanodots-modified films (Fig. 2B) is possible to observe the inclusion of the nanoparticles inside the cellulose structure, which should reflect further changes in characteristic material properties.

Mechanical behaviour of a material is usually represented by the elastic or Young modulus (Y), which is determined by the linear part of the strength-elongation film curves (Fig. 4S, Supplementary information). Young modulus values for the RC-based films are indicated in Table 1 (error interval determined from the slope error for each sample), and significant differences depending on the nano-dot embedded in the RC support film can be observed. Particularly, the inclusion of SiDs increases the film stiffness in around 100%, but it is around 70% and 40% in the case of CDs or N-CDs modified films. In this context, a significant increase in the elastic modulus of bacterial cellulose nanocomposites filled with Si NPs has already been reported (Yano, Maeda, Nakajima, Hagiwara, & Sawaguchi, 2008), while the presence of a high number of functional groups on the nanoparticles core in the case of N-CDs might reduce the effect on elastic resistance.

Changes in the electrical characteristic of the cellulosic nano-engineered films were determines by impedance spectroscopy measurements. The impedance, Z, is a complex number, Z = Zreal + j Zimg, which can be separated into real and imaginary parts by algebra rules. Zreal and Zimg components are related with the transport of charge across the system (electrical resistance, R) and charge storage (capacitance, C):
(1) Zreal = R/[1 + (ωRC)2] and Zimg = - ωR2C/[1 + (ωRC)2]
where ω = 2πf is the angular frequency. Electrical parameters can be determined from impedance plots by using equivalent circuit as models (Asaka, 1990; Benavente, García, Riley, Lozano, & de Abajo, 2000; Benavente, 2012), where the simplest case corresponds to a parallel association of a resistance and a capacitor (Benavente et al., 2000).

Fig. 3 shows the Bode plots, Zreal v. frequency (Fig. 3A), and - Zimg v. frequency in Fig. 3B) obtained for the studied films. Electrical conductivity (σ), a characteristic material parameter, is commonly determined from IS measurements by fitting the impedance and determining the electrical resistance (R) (Benavente, 2012): σ = Δx/R·S, where Δx and S represent the film thickness and cross-section, respectively. The values obtained for the different films are indicated in Table 1. According to IS results, the inclusion of nanodots increases the conductivity of the films (lower resistance values, and a shift to higher frequencies for the maximum of the imaginary impedance curve). SiDs and CDs reduces around 20–25 % the electrical resistance of the support film, but the inclusion of N-CDs nanodots (RC-4/N-CDs film) reduce it in more than 3 orders of magnitude. This result is compatible with the presence of higher number of negative charges on the surface of the nanodots due to their surface functional groups such as amines (CNH2) and amides (OCNH2), but differences in samples hydrophilicity (or water content) might also be considered. Conductivity increase can be a point of significant interest for applications of this nano-engineered film to electronic systems

Spectroscopic ellipsometry (SE) is a technique used for the characterization of thin films, since it allows to determine electrical/optical changes associated to both film surface and bulk phase modifications. Ellipsometry measures changes in light polarization due to its reflections or transmission across a solid structure, being the ellipsometry angles, Ψ and Δ, related with differential changes in amplitude and phase between light waves (tan(Ψ)eiΔ = rp/rs, where rp y rs are the amount of light reflected in the perpendicular and parallel planes of incidence) (Logothetidis, Gioti, & Gravalidis, 2003). Although SE measurements are usually carried out for silica or alumina films, a few numbers of papers have used this technique for estimation of thickness or optical characteristics of thin polymeric layers (Gioti, Laskarakis, & Logothetidis, 2004). Consequently, spectroscopic ellipsometry measurements of the original and modified RC-based films might give information on changes in both the real part of the complex refractive index (n) and the imaginary part of the complex refractive index (k) or extinction coefficient, associated to the different nanodots included in the cellulosic structure.

Fig. 4 shows a comparison of wavelength dependence of measured Ψ (Fig. 4A) and Δ (Fig. 4B) parameters for the different RC-based films, where interference fringes from multiple reflections at the back interface of the films (larger light penetration than film thickness) at high wavelength values exhibited by RC-4 and RC-4/CDs films are indication of the higher transparency of these samples (Gioti et al., 2004; Logothetidis et al., 2003). This point was confirmed by comparison of the curves obtained with the original RC-4 film and after striped its back surface (transparency reduction) (see Fig. S4, Supplementary material). As it can be observed in Fig. 4A, RC-4 and RC-4/CDs films show similar type of wavelength dependence, with a slight shift to higher wavelength values for the minimum of the curve for the RC-4/CDs-film when compared with the cellulosic support (from 265 to 325 nm). Both curves are like that previously reported for a polymeric substrate such as polyethylene terephthalate (PET) (Hilfiker et al., 2017) or poly(9-vinylcarbazole) (PVK) on a Si wafer, for the same range of wavelength (Alkano, El-Agez, Taya, & Afghjani, 2016).

Moreover, the observation of Fig. 4B also shows interesting information; although the curves obtained for all the samples exhibit a minimum between 230–250 nm, another minimum at 272 nm can also be observed for the RC-4/N-CDs film curve. This fact seems to indicate an important optical modification at that wavelength, causing a change in the material response associated to differences in the electrical vector phase by the presence of N-CDs nanodots.

From experimental Ψ and Δ values, using a Cauchy dispersion model relation (Fujiwara, 2007) to describe the dependence of the refractive index on the wavelength, RC-based films refraction index (n) and extinction coefficient (k) were determined. Values of n and k parameters at the wavelength commonly referred of 632.8 nm (Fujiwara, 2007) are indicated in Table 2 . These results are in good agreement with the values of the refractive index determined for the PVK sample (between 1.67 and 2.4) and a polysulfone layer deposited on a nano-porous alumina support (1.625 (Alkano et al., 2016; Ogieglo, Wormeerster, Wessling, & Benes, 2012). Moreover, differences in the extinction coefficient for the diverse cellulosic films have also been determined, and the reduction in k values when compared with the original RC-4 film could be caused by higher reflexion in the nanodots modified films. Consequently, the inclusion of nanodots in the structure of the cellulosic-support films modified their optical properties, opening the range of potential application to this field of research.

Another significant characteristic related with possible applications of the cellulose-based nano-engineered films is the transparency. For that reason, transmittance spectra in the 200–1000 nm wavelength region (UV-VIS) for RC-4 and RC-4/nanoparticles modified films were measured, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 . Transmittance values at both 632.8 nm and 1000 nm (maximum wavelength) are indicated in Table 2. As it can be observed, the inclusion of the different nano-dots in the cellulosic support slightly reduces its optical transmission: about 4% for SiDs and CDs RC-modified films, and 1.6% for the N-CDs film when the maximum value is considered. These values are in good agreement with those reported for a cellulose/CDs film (Zheng & Yan, 2015) prepared from microcrystalline cellulose power (around 89% for the cellulose film and 86% for cellulose/CDs), although our RC-4/N-CDs film exhibits a transparency ∼10% higher than the referred in that paper.

Furthermore, transmittance results also give other relevant information associated to the observed band at 272 nm for the RC-4/N-CDs film. According to non-classical band gaps (Wang et al., 2010), the observed absorption is assigned to π→π∗ transition, so the surface defect states must be accessed directly from the ground state, which are associated with carbonyl/hydroxyl groups and amine/amide functional groups present in the surface of the N-CDs nanodots studied in this work (Campos, Abellan et al., 2015; Campos, Algarra et al., 2015). It is interesting to remark that a peak at the same wavelength was already obtained for the RC-4/N-CDs film for the Δ parameter (Fig. 4B) determined from ellipsometry spectroscopy measurements.

This study presents a simple and environmentally friendly way of functionalizing regenerated cellulose films by the inclusion of different nanodots (SiDs, CDs and nitrogen-doped carbon dots or N-CDs). The modification increases (in different percentages) the mechanical resistance and electrical conductivity of the films depending on the selected nanodot, and it also affects characteristic optical parameters (refractive index and extinction coefficient), but hardly modified the high transparency of the cellulosic support film.

Results obtained for the film modified with N-CDs show its inherent fluorescent emission when analyzed by confocal fluorescence spectroscopy at different depths. It also exhibits high conductivity, attributed to the presence of higher number of charges on the nanodots surface due to their functional groups, and excellent transparency according to its high transmittance value (> 92%). Consequently, the non-toxic character of the support film and the N-CDs nanoparticles, the attained eco-friendly nano-engineering cellulosic-based film can be of interest for application in different kinds of systems such as optical and electronic devices, but also for biomedical systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: One of the authors (M.A.) thanks to the project CTQ2015-68951-C3-3-R (MINECO, Spain) and to ARDITI-Agência Regional para o Desenvolvimento da Investigação Tecnologia e Inovação is also acknowledged through the project M1420-01-0145-FEDER-000005-CQM+ (Madeira 14-20 Program). We also thank to the Supercomputing and Bioinnovation Center and Junta de Andalucía (PAI group FQM 258) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2018.10.074.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hybrid fluorescent nanoparticles from quantum dots coupled to cellulose nanocrystals
Inclusion of thiol DAB dendrimer/CdSe quantum dots based in a membrane structure: Surface and bulk membrane modification
Luminescent carbon nanoparticles: Effects of chemical functionalization, and evaluation of Ag+ sensing properties
Characterization of an engineered cellulose-based membrane by thiol dendrimer for heavy metals removal
Characterization of PVK polymeric material using rotating polarizer and analyzer ellipsomeeter with a speed ratio 1:1
Dielectric properties of cellulose acetate reverse osmosis membranes in aqueous salt solutions
Dendrimer as a multifunctional capping agent for metal nanoparticles for use in bioimaging, drug delivery and sensor applications

Use of impedance spectroscopy for characterization of membranes and the effect of different modifications
Inclusion of silver nanoparticles for improving regenerated cellulose membrane performance and reduction of biofouling
Tuneable light-emitting carbon-dot/polymer flexible films prepared through one-pot synthesis
Fluorescent chemosensor for pyridine based on N-doped carbon dots
Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
Fluorescent sensor for Cr(VI) based in functionalized silicon quantum dots with dendrimers
Characterization of cellulose membranes modified with luminescent silicon quantum dots nanoparticles
White-light-emitting polymer composite film based on carbon dots and lanthanide complexes
Cellulose-graphene quantum dot composite membranes using ionic liquid
3D network of cellulose-based energy storage devices and related emerging applications
Bio-based polymers with potential for biodegradability
IR-FUV ellipsometry studies on the optical, electronic and vibrational properties of polymeric membranes
Photoluminescent Hybrids of cellulose nanocrystals and carbon quantum dots as cytocompatible probes for in vitro bioimaging

Modification of cellulose nanofibrils with luminescent carbon dots
Nanocelluloses: A new family of nature‐based materials

Enhanced fluorescence properties of carbon dots in polymer films
Synthesis of luminescent carbon dots with ultrahigh quantum yield and inherent folate receptor-positive cancer cell targetability
Optical and electronic characterization on polymeric membranes
Self-assembly of nitrogen-doped carbon dots anchored on bacterial cellulose and their application in iron ion detection
Cellulose nanomaterials review: Structure, properties and nanocomposites
Spectroscopic ellipsometry analysis of a thin film composite membrane consisting of polysulfone on a porous α-alumina support
Powering the future: Application of cellulose-based materials for supercapacitors
One pot synthesis of carbon dots decorated carboxymethyl cellulose- hydroxyapatite nanocomposite for drug delivery, tissue engineering and Fe3+ ion sensing
Highly fluorescent N, S-co-doped carbon dots: Synthesis and multiple applications
The effect of N-doped quantum dots on the properties of in situ prepared colorless polyimide nanocomposite films
S- and N-doped carbon quantum dots: Surface chemistry dependent antibacterial activity
Development of micro- and nano-porous composite materials by processing cellulose with ionic liquids and supercritical CO2
Effect of lipid nanoparticles inclusion on transport parameters through regenerated cellulose membranes
Modification of regenerated cellulose membrane basedon thiol dendrimer
Bandgap-like strong fluorescence in functionalized carbon nanoparticles
